title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation

def:1.1.summary: Defines weak solution of the 3D NSE on $T^3$: v in $C^0(R$; $L^2)$ that is weakly divergence-free and satisfies the equation in the distributional sense against divergence-free test functions.

thm:1.2.summary: Main nonuniqueness result: there exists $\beta>0$ such that for any prescribed nonneg smooth energy profile e(t), there exists a weak NSE solution v in $C^0_t H^\beta_x$ whose kinetic energy equals e(t) for all t. Implies non-uniqueness in the Leray class.

thm:1.3.summary: Hölder-continuous dissipative Euler weak solutions arise as strong $L^2$ vanishing viscosity limits of finite-energy NSE weak solutions; the approximating sequence is constructed by the same convex integration scheme as thm:1.2.

prop:2.1.summary: Main iterative proposition: given a triple $(v_q, p_q, R_q)$ satisfying the NSE-Reynolds system and inductive estimates at level q, there exists a next triple $(v_{q+1}, p_{q+1}, R_{q+1})$ satisfying the same estimates at level q+1, with $||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le$ M $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$. The entire proof of thm:1.2 and thm:1.3 reduces to iterating this proposition.

prop:3.1.summary: Beltrami wave construction: for rational directions $\xi$, defines vector fields $W(x) = \Sigma a_\xi B_\xi e^{i\lambda\xi\cdotx}$ that are real, divergence-free, and satisfy div(W⊗$W) = \nabla(|W|^2/2)$, enabling stress cancellation in the NSE-Reynolds system.

prop:3.2.summary: Geometric amplitude decomposition: for any R near the identity in the space of $3\times3$ symmetric matrices, constructs disjoint sets $\Lambda_\alpha \subset S^2\capQ^3$ and smooth amplitude functions $\gamma_\xi^(\alpha)(R)$ such that R $= (1/2) \Sigma_{\xi\in\Lambda_\alpha} \gamma_\xi^(\alpha)(R)^2 (Id - \xi$⊗$\xi)$. This is the mechanism for canceling the Reynolds stress.

rem:3.3.summary: Specifies the number $N_\Lambda$ of direction sets needed in prop:3.2, which constrains the divisibility condition on the base frequency parameter a in prop:2.1.

prop:3.4.summary: Intermittent Beltrami wave stress identity: the intermittent wave $W_{(\xi)}(x,t) (localized$ to a sparse spatial grid of scale r) satisfies $\Sigma \gamma_{(\xi)}(R)^2 \int W_{(\xi)}$ ⊗ $W_{(-\xi)}$ dx = R, enabling cancellation of the Reynolds stress R via the amplitude functions from prop:3.2.

prop:3.5.summary: $L^p$ derivative bounds for intermittent Beltrami waves $W_{(\xi)}$: $||\nabla^N \partial_t^K W_{(\xi)}||_{L^p}$ ≲ $\lambda^N (\lambda\sigmar\mu)^K r^{3/2-3/p}$, tracking the wave's frequency $(\lambda)$, spatial concentration (r), temporal oscillation $(\mu)$, and lattice spacing $(\sigma)$.

lem:3.7.summary: $L^p$ decorrelation/independence lemma: for a slow function f (Lipschitz scale $\lambda)$ and a fast periodic function g (period $1/\kappa \gg 1/\lambda), ||fg||_{L^p}$ ≲ $C_f ||g||_{L^p}$. Used to estimate products of the slowly-varying amplitude with the rapidly-oscillating Beltrami waves.

lem:4.1.summary: Stress cutoff finiteness and amplitude bounds: $i_max(q)$ is finite (only finitely many cutoff levels are active), $\rho_i$ ≲ ℓ^{-1}, and the key summation $\Sigma \rho_i^{1/2} 2^{-i} \le 3\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$, controlling the total $L^2$ contribution from all cutoff levels.

lem:4.2.summary: Bounds for the stress cutoff functions $\chi_{(i)}$: $||\chi_{(i)}||_{L^2}$ ≲ 2^{-i} and $||\chi_{(i)}||_{C^N_{x,t}}$ ≲ ℓ^{-N} for $N\ge1$, establishing that cutoffs are localized in space and regular at scale ℓ.

lem:4.3.summary: Energy cutoff estimates: the zeroth-level cutoff $\chi_{(0)}$ is large $(\int \chi_{(0)}^2 \ge |T^3|/2$, ensuring the energy iterate works), while higher-level contributions $\Sigma_{i\ge1} \rho_i \int \chi_{(i)}^2$ ≲ $\lambda_q^{-\varepsilon_R} \delta_{q+1}$ are negligible.

lem:4.4.summary: Amplitude coefficient $a_{(\xi)}$ bounds: $||a_{(\xi)}||_{L^2}$ ≲ $\rho_i^{1/2} 2^{-i}$ ≲ $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}, ||a_{(\xi)}||_{L^\infty}$ ≲ $\rho_i^{1/2}$ ≲ $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2} 2^i$, and $C^N$ bounds ≲ ℓ^{-N}. These amplitudes drive the velocity perturbation.

prop:4.5.summary: Full perturbation estimates: the principal part $w^{(p)}_{q+1}$, incompressibility corrector $w^{(c)}_{q+1}$, and temporal corrector $w^{(t)}_{q+1}$ satisfy sharp $L^2, W^{1,p}, \partial_t L^p$, and $C^N$ bounds in terms of $\delta_{q+1}, \lambda_{q+1}$, r, $\sigma, \mu$, ℓ. This is the central technical proposition for the perturbation step.

cor:4.6.summary: Combines prop:4.5 into bounds on the total velocity increment $w_{q+1} = w^{(p)} + w^{(c)} + w^{(t)}$: $||w_{q+1}||_{L^2} \le (3M/4) \delta_{q+1}^{1/2}, ||v_{q+1} - v_q||_{L^2} \le$ M $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$, plus $W^{1,p}$ and $C^N$ estimates. These feed directly into prop:2.1.

prop:5.1.summary: Reynolds stress reduction: for suitable p>1 and $\varepsilon_R>0 (depending$ only on b and $\beta)$, the new Reynolds stress R̃ satisfies ||R̃$||_{L^p}$ ≲ $\lambda_{q+1}^{-2\varepsilon_R} \delta_{q+2}$. This is the key estimate showing the Reynolds stress shrinks at each iteration step.

cor:5.2.summary: NSE-Reynolds closure: upgrades prop:5.1 to produce a traceless symmetric tensor $R_{q+1}$ satisfying both $||R_{q+1}||_{L^1} \le \lambda_{q+1}^{-\varepsilon_R} \delta_{q+2}$ and $||R_{q+1}||_{C^1_{x,t}} \le \lambda_{q+1}^{10}$, completing the inductive step for prop:2.1.

lem:6.1.summary: Energy continuity: for $|t-t'| \le 2$ℓ, the prescribed energy e(t), the $L^2$ norm of $v_q$, the cutoff integrals $\int \chi_i^2$ dx, and the density $\rho(t)$ all vary by at most O(ℓ^{1/2}). Controls temporal fluctuations in the energy matching step.

lem:6.2.summary: Energy tracking when $\rho_0(t) \ne 0$: the prescribed energy profile e(t) and the kinetic energy of $v_{q+1}(t)$ agree to within $\delta_{q+2}/4$, i.e., $|e(t) - \int|v_{q+1}|^2$ dx $- \delta_{q+2}/2| \le \delta_{q+2}/4$, enabling the inductive energy estimate to close.

lem:6.3.summary: Energy tracking when $\rho_0(t) = 0$: the iterate trivializes $(v_{q+1}$ ≡ 0, $R_{q+1}$ ≡ 0) and the energy gap $e(t) - \int|v_{q+1}|^2 \le (3/4)\delta_{q+2}$, maintaining the inductive bound trivially.

lem:B.1.summary: Anti-divergence commutator estimate: for slowly-varying a (scale $\lambda \le \kappa)$ and a high-frequency function ℙ$_{\ge\kappa}f$, the inverse-divergence $|\nabla|^{-1}(a$ ℙ$_{\ge\kappa}$ f) satisfies an $L^p$ bound ≲ $C_a (1 + \lambda^L/\kappa^{L-2}) ||f||_{L^p}/\kappa$. Used to estimate the commutator/oscillation error in Section 5.

edges.note: thm:1.2 is proved by iterating prop:2.1 starting from $(v_0, p_0, 0)$ and taking the $L^2$ limit; the energy prescription is encoded in the initial data and $\rho_0$
thm:1.3 uses the same iterative scheme as thm:1.2 but with viscosity parameter $\nu_n \to 0$, producing a sequence converging strongly to the given Euler solution
The vanishing viscosity construction is a variant of the nonuniqueness construction; same scheme, different parameter regime
prop:2.1 requires $||v_{q+1}-v_q||_{L^2} \le$ M $\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$, which is exactly (4.46) from cor:4.6
prop:2.1 requires the inductive Reynolds stress bounds at level q+1; these are the $L^1$ and $C^1$ estimates of cor:5.2
Energy inductive estimate at level q+1 (eq:2.6) when $\rho_0 \ne 0$ follows from lem:6.2
Energy inductive estimate at level q+1 when $\rho_0 = 0$ follows from lem:6.3
cor:4.6 combines the individual component estimates of prop:4.5 into total perturbation bounds
cor:5.2 upgrades prop:5.1's $L^p$ stress bound to the required $L^1$ and $C^1$ bounds via interpolation and the $C^N$ estimates from cor:4.6
$L^2$ bound for $w^{(p)}_{q+1}$ uses lem:4.4 amplitude bounds and prop:3.5 wave estimates via lem:3.7
$W^{1,p}$ and $C^N$ estimates for the velocity perturbation use the $L^p$ derivative bounds for the intermittent Beltrami waves
Amplitude $a_{(\xi)}$ bounds inherit $C^N$ estimates from the cutoff $\chi_{(i)}$ via lem:4.2
The key summation bound $\Sigma \rho_i^{1/2} 2^{-i} \le 3\delta_{q+1}^{1/2}$ from lem:4.1 is used to bound $||a_{(\xi)}||_{L^2}$
Reynolds stress reduction uses $L^p$ estimates for the waves (linear error, corrector error, oscillation error all bounded via prop:3.5)
The oscillation error in Section 5.3 uses the $L^p$ decorrelation lemma to separate slow (amplitude) from fast (wave) factors
The oscillation/commutator error (Section 5.3) applies the anti-divergence estimate lem:B.1 to the product of the amplitude and the projected stress
The stress cancellation (main term in prop:5.1) uses prop:3.4's identity $\Sigma \gamma_{(\xi)}^2 \int W_{(\xi)}$⊗$W_{(-\xi)} =$ R to absorb the Reynolds stress $R_q$
The amplitude functions $\gamma_{(\xi)}(R)$ and the direction sets $\Lambda_\alpha$ in prop:3.4 are the ones constructed in prop:3.2
The intermittent Beltrami wave $W_{(\xi)}$ is built from the Beltrami flow structure of prop:3.1, inheriting div(W⊗$W) = \nabla(|W|^2/2)$
$L^p$ estimates for $W_{(\xi)}$ in prop:3.5 apply to the intermittent Beltrami waves defined in prop:3.4
Energy continuity lem:6.1 is used to control temporal fluctuations of e(t) and $||v_q||_{L^2}^2$ in the energy matching argument of lem:6.2
The lower bound $\int \chi_{(0)}^2 \ge |T^3|/2$ from lem:4.3 is needed to ensure the energy matching via $\rho_0$ is non-degenerate when $\rho_0 \ne 0$
The condition that a is a multiple of $N_\Lambda (from$ rem:3.3) is a hypothesis of prop:2.1, ensuring the Beltrami direction sets tile $T^3$ compatibly

foundations.description: Basic definitions and geometric building blocks for the convex integration scheme

wave_construction.description: Intermittent Beltrami waves: definition, stress identity, $L^p$ estimates, and decorrelation. The intermittency (spatial concentration parameter r $\ll 1)$ is what makes the construction work for NSE (unlike the Euler case).

perturbation_construction.description: Building the velocity increment $w_{q+1}$ from stress cutoffs, amplitude coefficients, and Beltrami waves; establishing all required bounds

stress_reduction.description: Showing the new Reynolds stress $R_{q+1}$ is smaller than $R_q$ by factor $\lambda_{q+1}^{-\varepsilon_R}$; the heart of why the iteration converges

energy_matching.description: Ensuring the iterates track the prescribed energy profile e(t); the intermittency parameter r is calibrated to keep energy errors within the inductive $\delta_{q+2}$ budget

main_iteration.description: The single inductive proposition that packages all the above into one step: $(v_q, R_q) \to (v_{q+1}, R_{q+1})$ with improved estimates

main_results.description: The two main theorems: nonuniqueness of finite-energy NSE solutions, and Euler solutions as vanishing viscosity limits

Leray34.title: Sur le mouvement d'un liquide visqueux emplissant l'espace
Leray34.note: 1934 foundational paper proving existence of finite-energy weak NSE solutions; thm:1.2 directly contradicts uniqueness in this class

DLSZ09.title: The Euler equations as a differential inclusion
DLSZ09.note: Introduces convex integration for fluid equations; foundational predecessor of this paper's method

DLSZ13.title: Dissipative continuous Euler flows
DLSZ13.note: Key predecessor in the Onsager/Euler nonuniqueness program

DSZ17.title: Non-uniqueness and h-principle for Hölder-continuous weak solutions of the Euler equations
DSZ17.note: Closest Euler analogue; this paper extends the technique to NSE with intermittent waves

Isett16.title: A proof of Onsager's conjecture
Isett16.note: Proves Onsager $C^{1/3}$ threshold for Euler; the NSE result here uses a different (lower) Hölder regularity but the same h-principle philosophy

BDLSV17.title: Onsager's conjecture for admissible weak solutions
BDLSV17.note: Concurrent work in the same program; admissible Euler solutions below $C^{1/3}$

BMV17.title: Unpublished work on NSE non-uniqueness from smooth data
BMV17.note: Successor work extending to smooth initial data; blocked in queue as prerequisite context

GuillodSverak17.title: Numerical investigations of non-uniqueness for the Navier-Stokes initial value problem in borderline spaces
GuillodSverak17.note: Numerical evidence for nonuniqueness; cited as motivation alongside this paper's analytic proof

JiaSverak15.title: Are the incompressible 3d Navier-Stokes equations locally ill-posed in the natural energy space?
JiaSverak15.note: Ill-posedness result in energy space, related context

CaffarelliKohnNirenberg82.title: Partial regularity of suitable weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations
CaffarelliKohnNirenberg82.note: CKN partial regularity theorem for suitable weak solutions
